pages.title: Overview
pages.body:
A single sphere cycles through five lighting rigs every 4 seconds. Each mode uses different Three.js light types — from the simplest ambient-only fill to a full three-point studio rig.

The key data is the MODES array and the CYCLE_DURATION constant at the top of the file.

pages.title: clearLights()
pages.body:
clearLights() removes every light added in the previous mode and disposes it. Disposing lights frees their GPU resources — always dispose Three.js objects you're done with.

The pattern of storing scene objects in ctx._lights and then clearing them here is how Artlab examples manage dynamic scene content.

pages.title: applyMode() — all five rigs
pages.body:
Each branch of applyMode() builds a different lighting rig:

· idx 0 — AmbientLight only (flat, no depth)
· idx 1 — Directional + dim ambient (sunlight)
· idx 2 — Orbiting PointLight (stored for animation)
· idx 3 — Three-point studio (key, fill, rim)
· idx 4 — HemisphereLight (sky + ground bounce)

Compare how the sphere reads differently under each mode in the preview.

pages.title: setup()
pages.body: setup() creates a single grey sphere and starts mode 0. The sphere's roughness: 0.5 and metalness: 0.1 make it a good neutral test subject — reflective enough to show highlights, matte enough to show diffuse shading.

pages.title: update() — cycling
pages.body:
update() compares elapsed time against _lastSwitch. When 4 seconds have passed it increments _modeIdx (wrapping via modulo) and calls applyMode().

The point-light mode (idx 2) also animates the light in a circle each frame using sin/cos of elapsed time.
